Describe where the holding stands, using the present perfect with "for" and "since" at least twice (for example, "We have worked with them since 2015") and the passive voice once (for example, "the return has been filed").

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1We have worked with this firm since 2015.Nós trabalhamos com esta firma desde 2015.
2The tax return has been filed before the deadline.A declaração foi entregue antes do prazo.
3Are these costs deductible this fiscal year?Estes custos são dedutíveis neste ano fiscal?
4How long have you used this accounting system?Há quanto tempo você usa este sistema contábil?
5The statement has not been reconciled yet.O extrato ainda não foi conciliado.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Making Proposals -- Pitching Ideas to the Board

Pitch an idea to your partners in English: lead with the rationale, keep the scope tight, and propose a low-risk pilot with a clear value proposition. Use proposing frames ("I would like to suggest that we...", "Why don't we...?", "What if we tested it?", "I propose that we...") and report the outcome in the passive voice (the proposal was approved, the budget has been allocated). New vocabulary: pitch, rationale, scope, rollout, pilot, buy-in, value proposition, deliverable, viable, streamline, incentive, secure.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pitch -- to present an idea to people in order to win their support
"On Thursday I will pitch the idea to the board." (apresentar / fazer um pitch)
Rationale -- the set of reasons that explain why an idea makes sense
"Lead with the rationale before any numbers." (justificativa)
Scope -- how large a project is -- what it includes and leaves out
"Keep the scope tight: one site, not all of them." (escopo)
Rollout -- the full launch of a plan or system to all users or sites
"A full rollout is risky, so we will start smaller." (implantação total)
Pilot -- a small first test of an idea before a full rollout
"What if we ran a three-month pilot at one site?" (projeto piloto)
Buy-in -- the agreement and support of the people who must approve a plan
"Secure buy-in from David before the meeting." (adesão / apoio)
Value proposition -- a short, clear statement of the benefit the partner gets
"Our value proposition: same output, lower cost." (proposta de valor)
Deliverable -- a concrete result a project must produce by a set date
"Each phase has a clear deliverable and a deadline." (entregável)
Viable -- realistic enough to work in practice and be worth doing
"The pilot shows the plan is viable and low-risk." (viável)
Streamline -- to make a process simpler, faster, and more efficient
"The plan would streamline approvals across both sites." (otimizar / simplificar)
Incentive -- a reason or reward that motivates someone to act or agree
"The cost saving is a strong incentive for the partners." (incentivo)
Secure -- to obtain or lock in something important, such as support or funding
"We need to secure approval and the budget." (garantir / assegurar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Pitch
Rationale
Scope
Rollout
Pilot
Buy-in
Value proposition
Deliverable
Viable
Streamline
Incentive
Secure

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On Thursday I pitch my idea to the partners. I will lead with the rationale: the order process is slow and it costs us margin. Then I propose the plan. I would like to suggest that we run a three-month pilot at one site, with clear deliverables. What if we measured the cost saving before we scale? I propose that we keep the scope tight, because the board gets nervous with a full rollout. The value proposition is simple: the same output at a lower cost. That is the incentive, and the plan is viable and low-risk. If the pitch goes well, the proposal will be approved and the budget will be allocated -- and last year a similar pilot was approved in a single meeting. My job is to secure the partners' buy-in.

1. How does the writer introduce the pilot?
A "I would like to suggest that we run a pilot." -- a proposing frame.
B "We will run a pilot, end of story."
C "Maybe a pilot, I don't know."
2. Which tense follows "What if we..."?
A The present: "What if we measure".
B The past simple: "What if we measured".
C The infinitive: "What if we to measure".
3. How is the outcome reported -- "a similar pilot ___ approved"?
A "was approved" -- passive: was + past participle.
B "approved" -- with no "be".
C "is approving".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Proposing Frames & the Passive

GRAMMAR

Para propor uma ideia, use frames claros: "I would like to suggest THAT we...", "I would recommend + -ing", "Why don't we...?", "What if we + passado?", "I propose THAT we...". Para relatar o resultado, use a voz passiva (be / was / have been + partícipio): "the proposal was approved", "the budget has been allocated". / To propose an idea, use clear frames; to report the outcome, use the passive (be / was / have been + past participle).

FunctionFormExample
Suggest / propose+ that + clause"I propose that we run a pilot."
Recommend+ -ing (or that)"I recommend starting smaller."
Float an ideaWhat if we + past simple"What if we tested it?"
Report the outcomebe / was / have been + participle"The proposal was approved."

Key point / Ponto-chave: proponha na voz ativa com um frame ("I propose that we..."), mas relate o resultado na passiva ("the proposal WAS approved", "the budget HAS BEEN allocated") -- a passiva SEMPRE precisa do BE. / Propose in the active voice with a frame, but report the result in the passive -- the passive always needs "be" (was / were / been).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I would like to suggest we run a pilot."
"I would recommend with one site."
"What if we the results after three months?"
"The proposal was at the meeting."
"The budget has been for next year."
"We need to the partners' buy-in."

Stage 2: Order the Pitch

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a strong pitch in the correct order.

?You lower the risk: instead of a full rollout, you suggest a pilot at one site, with a short timeline and clear deliverables.
?You open with the rationale: you explain why the current process is a problem and why it matters now.
?You ask for the decision: you secure buy-in, confirm the next step, and agree on when the results will be reviewed.
?You state what you are proposing, in one clear line, and you keep the scope tight.
?You make the case on value: you give the return on the investment and a simple value proposition.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I would like to suggest that we run a pilot first.
Eu gostaria de sugerir que façamos um piloto primeiro.
What if we tested it as a pilot first?
E se testássemos como um piloto primeiro?
Our value proposition is the same output at a lower cost.
Nossa proposta de valor é a mesma produção a um custo menor.
The proposal was approved and the budget has been allocated.
A proposta foi aprovada e o orçamento foi alocado.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to propose a pilot to the board in a formal way. Which is best?
A "I propose that we run a three-month pilot at one site."
B "I propose to running a pilot maybe."
C "We do a pilot, ok?"
The board said yes. How do you report it naturally?
A "The proposal approved by the board."
B "The proposal was approved by the board."
C "The proposal is approving."
You want to float a tentative idea without committing. Which fits?
A "What if we tested it at one site first?"
B "What if we test it at one site first?"
C "What if to test it at one site?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are pitching an idea to your partners. Open with the rationale, propose a pilot using a frame (for example, "I would like to suggest that we..." or "I propose that we..."), give the value proposition in one line, and then report a likely outcome in the passive voice (for example, "the proposal was approved" or "the budget has been allocated").

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1I would like to suggest that we run a pilot first.Eu gostaria de sugerir que façamos um piloto primeiro.
2The rationale is simple: it would streamline the process.A justificativa é simples: isso otimizaria o processo.
3What if we tested it as a pilot first?E se testássemos como um piloto primeiro?
4Our value proposition is the same output at a lower cost.Nossa proposta de valor é a mesma produção a um custo menor.
5The proposal was approved and the budget has been allocated.A proposta foi aprovada e o orçamento foi alocado.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

The Big Review -- Block 2 Integration

Review and integrate everything from Block 2 in one full negotiation: report results with the present perfect, state obligations with shall / must / may, describe numbers with trend language, give advice with should / had better, and compare fairly with whereas / unlike / different from. Key words: milestone, obligation, warranty, revenue, deadline, track record, option, trade-off, rapport, mindset.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Review each key word from Block 2 and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Milestone -- an important stage or achievement in a project
"Closing the third deal was a real milestone this year." (Marco)
Obligation -- a duty or requirement you are bound to fulfill
"Each party has an obligation to deliver on time." (Obrigação)
Warranty -- a written promise to repair or replace a product
"The contract must include a twelve-month warranty." (Garantia)
Revenue -- the total income a company earns from its business
"Revenue has risen sharply this quarter." (Receita / faturamento)
Deadline -- the latest date or time by which something must be done
"We had better confirm the deadline early." (Prazo)
Track record -- a record of past performance and results
"We have built a strong track record together." (Histórico de resultados)
Option -- a choice or course of action that is available
"We should weigh the options before we decide." (Opção)
Trade-off -- a balance where you gain one thing but lose another
"A lower price is a trade-off for a longer contract." (Compensação / troca)
Rapport -- a warm, easy connection and mutual understanding
"A short comment on art helps build rapport." (Sintonia / boa relação)
Mindset -- an established way of thinking and approaching things
"Keep your warm mindset and add American directness." (Mentalidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Milestone
Obligation
Warranty
Revenue
Deadline
Track record
Option
Trade-off
Rapport
Mindset

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Block 2 brought five tools together. Before the final negotiation, Maria Claudia reports her track record with the present perfect: "we have closed three deals, and revenue has risen sharply." She states the obligations with modals: "each party shall deliver, the contract must include a warranty, and either side may request a change." She describes the numbers with trend language, and gives advice with had better: "we had better confirm the deadline today." Finally, she compares the two styles fairly: "they are direct, whereas we build rapport first; their urgency is different from ours" -- the preposition is always "from", never "of". With a clear option and a fair trade-off on the table, she is ready to close the deal.

1. Why does the text use "have closed" and "has risen" instead of the simple past?
A The period is still open and the result matters now (present perfect).
B The events happened on a fixed past date.
C They are commands.
2. What follows the modal in "each party shall deliver"?
A "to" + verb ("shall to deliver").
B The base verb, with no "to" ("shall deliver").
C The past participle.
3. Which preposition is correct after "different"?
A "from" (different from ours).
B "of" (different of ours).
C "than" (different than ours).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Block 2 Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Esta aula revisa as cinco estruturas do Bloco 2 juntas. PRESENT PERFECT (have + particípio) = resultado até agora. MODAIS shall/must/may + verbo base (sem "to") = obrigação e permissão. TREND LANGUAGE (verbo + advérbio, ex.: rose sharply) = descrever números. ADVICE should/ought to/had better = conselho (had better tem consequência). COMPARISON whereas/while + oração, unlike + substantivo, different FROM. / This lesson reviews all five Block 2 structures together. PRESENT PERFECT (have + past participle) = result up to now. MODALS shall/must/may + base verb (no "to") = obligation and permission. TREND LANGUAGE (verb + adverb) = describe numbers. ADVICE should/ought to/had better = recommend; had better has a consequence. COMPARISON whereas/while + clause, unlike + noun, different FROM.

StructureUseExample
Present perfectresult up to now"we have closed three deals"
Modals (shall/must/may)obligation / permission (+ base verb)"each party shall deliver"
Trend languagedescribe numbers"revenue rose sharply"
Advice (should / had better)recommend; consequence"we had better confirm the deadline"
Comparison (whereas / unlike / different from)contrast fairly"they are direct, whereas we build rapport"

Key point / Ponto-chave: com período aberto (this quarter), use o present perfect, não o simple past; modal vem com verbo base (sem "to"); e a preposição é sempre "different from". / With an open period (this quarter), use the present perfect, not the simple past; a modal takes the base verb (no "to"); and the preposition is always "different from".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We closed three deals this quarter."
"Each party deliver on time."
"Revenue rose this quarter."
"We had confirm the deadline today."
"Their urgency is different ours."
"A lower price is a fair for a longer contract."

Stage 2: Lead the Negotiation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of leading the negotiation in the correct order.

?You present the numbers with trend language, such as revenue has risen sharply.
?You report your track record with the present perfect: we have closed three deals.
?You compare the two cultures fairly with whereas and unlike, and adapt your approach.
?You state the obligations with modals: each party shall deliver, the contract must include a warranty.
?You give a clear recommendation with should or had better about the deadline.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We have closed three deals this quarter.
Fechamos três negócios neste trimestre.
Each party shall deliver, and the contract must include a warranty.
Cada parte deverá entregar, e o contrato deve incluir uma garantia.
We had better confirm the deadline today.
É melhor confirmarmos o prazo hoje.
They are direct, whereas we build rapport; their pace is different from ours.
Eles são diretos, enquanto nós criamos sintonia; o ritmo deles é diferente do nosso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to report a result in a period that is still open. Which is best?
A "We closed three deals this quarter."
B "We have closed three deals this quarter."
C "We are closing three deals this quarter."
You want to state a contractual obligation. Which is best?
A "The contract must include a warranty."
B "The contract must to include a warranty."
C "The contract should included a warranty."
You want to contrast the two negotiating styles. Which is best?
A "Their pace is different of ours."
B "They are direct, whereas we build rapport first."
C "Unlike they are direct, we build rapport first."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Self-assessment of Block 2: in five sentences, report one result with the present perfect, state one obligation with a modal, describe one trend, give one recommendation with should or had better, and compare your style with a US partner's using whereas or different from.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1We have closed three deals this quarter.Fechamos três negócios neste trimestre.
2Each party shall deliver, and the contract must include a warranty.Cada parte deverá entregar, e o contrato deve incluir uma garantia.
3Revenue has risen sharply this quarter.A receita subiu acentuadamente neste trimestre.
4We had better confirm the deadline today.É melhor confirmarmos o prazo hoje.
5They are direct, whereas we build rapport; their pace is different from ours.Eles são diretos, enquanto nós criamos sintonia; o ritmo deles é diferente do nosso.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Houston, We Have Culture -- US vs Brazil

Compare business and art culture across the US and Brazil. Contrast two ideas with whereas and while (same meaning, whereas is a little more formal), mark a clear difference with unlike + a noun, and soften with similar to and different from -- the preposition is always "from". New vocabulary: punctuality, hierarchy, directness, formality, etiquette, gesture, acquaintance, rapport, custom, mindset.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Punctuality -- the habit of being exactly on time
"American meetings value punctuality; they start at nine sharp." (Pontualidade)
Hierarchy -- the system of ranks and authority in an organization
"A flat hierarchy lets a junior analyst speak up freely." (Hierarquia)
Directness -- the quality of saying what you mean openly and early
"Americans often appreciate directness in a negotiation." (Franqueza / objetividade)
Formality -- the degree of official, polite behavior expected
"Brazilian first meetings often carry more formality." (Formalidade)
Etiquette -- the accepted rules of polite behavior in a culture
"Email etiquette in the US is informal in tone but precise." (Etiqueta / boas maneiras)
Gesture -- a movement of the hands or body that carries meaning
"A firm handshake is a positive gesture in US business." (Gesto)
Acquaintance -- a person you know slightly, not a close friend
"At the trade show I met a useful acquaintance from Houston." (Conhecido / a)
Rapport -- a warm, easy connection and mutual understanding
"A short comment on art helps build rapport quickly." (Sintonia / boa relação)
Custom -- a usual, traditional way of doing something in a culture
"Building the relationship first is a Brazilian custom." (Costume)
Mindset -- an established way of thinking and approaching things
"Keep your warm mindset and add a little US punctuality." (Mentalidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Punctuality
Hierarchy
Directness
Formality
Etiquette
Gesture
Acquaintance
Rapport
Custom
Mindset

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before her trip to Houston, Maria Claudia studies the local custom. US meetings value punctuality and directness: they start exactly on time and state the point early, whereas Brazilian meetings often build rapport first with a little small talk. Unlike a traditional firm, the Houston office has a flat hierarchy, so a junior may openly disagree with a director. The email etiquette is informal in tone but precise, which is different from the more formal Brazilian opening -- notice that the preposition is always "from", never "of" or "than". The American sense of humor is similar to hers, while the sense of personal space is not. Her plan is wise: keep her warm mindset and her custom of building rapport, but adapt to their punctuality and respect their etiquette, from a firm handshake to the right gesture when she disagrees.

1. Which word does the text use to contrast two full ideas (US vs Brazilian meetings)?
A "whereas" (and "while") -- they join two clauses to contrast them.
B "unlike" -- it joins two clauses.
C "similar to" -- it shows a difference.
2. What follows "unlike" in "unlike a traditional firm"?
A A full clause with subject and verb.
B A noun ("a traditional firm").
C An adjective only.
3. Which preposition is correct after "different"?
A "from" (different from ours).
B "of" (different of ours).
C "than" (different than ours).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparing & Contrasting

GRAMMAR

Para comparar duas culturas, use conectores e preposições certas. WHEREAS e WHILE + oração contrastam duas ideias completas (mesmo sentido; whereas é um pouco mais formal). UNLIKE + substantivo marca uma diferença clara e oposta. Para comparações mais suaves, SIMILAR TO + substantivo (parecido) e DIFFERENT FROM + substantivo (diferente). Atenção à preposição: é sempre different FROM, nunca "of" nem "than". / To compare two cultures, use the right connectors and prepositions. WHEREAS and WHILE + clause contrast two full ideas (same meaning; whereas is more formal). UNLIKE + noun marks a clear, opposite difference. For softer comparisons, SIMILAR TO + noun and DIFFERENT FROM + noun. The preposition is always different FROM -- never "of" or "than".

FormUseExample
whereas + clausecontrast two ideas (formal)"we warm up, whereas they start on time"
while + clausesame meaning, softer"they are direct, while we build rapport"
unlike + nouna clear, opposite difference"unlike Brazil, the hierarchy is flat"
similar to + nounsofter: alike"their humor is similar to ours"
different from + nounsofter: not alike (always from)"the etiquette is different from ours"

Key point / Ponto-chave: whereas/while ligam duas orações; unlike vem antes de substantivo. A preposição é sempre "different from": "their food is different of" → "their food is different from ours". / whereas/while join two clauses; unlike takes a noun. The preposition is always "different from", never "of" or "than".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word, connector, or preposition. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"US meetings start on time, ours warm up first."
" a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."
"Their taste in art is similar ours."
"Their email etiquette is different ours."
"A short comment on art helps build ."
"I will keep my warm and add a little US punctuality."

Stage 2: Compare Cultures in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of comparing cultures fairly in the correct order.

?You mark a strong contrast with unlike, when one culture does the opposite of the other.
?You observe the local custom before you judge it, so you compare fairly.
?You adapt your own mindset where it helps, while you keep your own strengths, such as warmth.
?You name one clear difference using whereas or while, such as punctuality.
?You soften the comparison with similar to and different from, because most differences are partial.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

US meetings start on time, whereas ours warm up first.
Reuniões nos EUA começam no horário, enquanto as nossas se aquecem primeiro.
Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat.
Diferente de uma empresa tradicional, a hierarquia deles é horizontal.
Their email etiquette is different from ours.
A etiqueta de e-mail deles é diferente da nossa.
I keep my custom of building rapport, and I respect their etiquette.
Mantenho meu costume de criar sintonia e respeito a etiqueta deles.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to contrast two full ideas about meetings. Which is best?
A "Unlike they start on time, we warm up first."
B "They start on time, whereas we warm up first."
C "Different from they start on time, we warm up."
You want to say their etiquette is not the same as ours. Which is best?
A "Their etiquette is different from ours."
B "Their etiquette is different of ours."
C "Their etiquette is different than ours."
You want to mark a clear difference about hierarchy. Which is best?
A "Unlike their hierarchy is flat, ours is strong."
B "Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."
C "Similar to a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Compare US and Brazilian business culture in four sentences: contrast one habit with whereas or while, mark one clear difference with unlike + a noun, name one thing similar to and one different from Brazil, and say what you would keep and what you would adapt.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1US meetings start on time, whereas ours warm up first.Reuniões nos EUA começam no horário, enquanto as nossas se aquecem primeiro.
2Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat.Diferente de uma empresa tradicional, a hierarquia deles é horizontal.
3Their taste in art is similar to ours.O gosto deles por arte é parecido com o nosso.
4Their email etiquette is different from ours.A etiqueta de e-mail deles é diferente da nossa.
5I keep my custom of building rapport, and I respect their etiquette.Mantenho meu costume de criar sintonia e respeito a etiqueta deles.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...

Recommend and decide in a meeting: should and ought to for normal advice (same meaning, ought to is more formal), and had better for strong advice with a real consequence. After should and had better, use the base verb with no "to". New vocabulary: option, weigh the options, pros and cons, recommend, alternative, prioritize, downside, trade-off, postpone, reach a consensus.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Option -- one of the possible choices in a decision
"We have three options: expand, wait, or run a pilot." (Opção)
Weigh the options -- to compare the choices carefully before deciding
"Let us weigh the options against our cash position." (Avaliar / ponderar as opções)
Pros and cons -- the advantages and the disadvantages of a choice
"Each path has its pros and cons; let us list them." (Prós e contras)
Recommend -- to advise a choice as the best one to take
"I recommend the safe path; it protects our margins." (Recomendar)
Alternative -- another option you could choose instead
"If we do not expand, the alternative is a small pilot." (Alternativa)
Prioritize -- to rank choices and decide what comes first
"We should prioritize the option that protects the partnership." (Priorizar)
Downside -- the negative side or disadvantage of a choice
"The downside of expanding now is the high cost." (Desvantagem / ponto negativo)
Trade-off -- a balance: you accept one loss to gain something else
"The trade-off of the safe path is that we move slower." (Compensação / troca)
Postpone -- to delay something to a later date
"We could postpone the expansion until next quarter." (Adiar)
Reach a consensus -- to arrive at an agreement the whole group accepts
"Let us reach a consensus before the call with David." (Chegar a um consenso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Option
Weigh the options
Pros and cons
Recommend
Alternative
Prioritize
Downside
Trade-off
Postpone
Reach a consensus

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The decision meeting has three options on the table. Before we choose, we should weigh the options and list the pros and cons of each path. We ought to prioritize the choice that protects the partnership -- should and ought to mean the same here, but ought to sounds a little more formal. The downside of expanding now is the cost, so we had better not commit the full budget at once. The alternative is to postpone and invest in the current line; the trade-off is that we move slower. But the supplier needs an answer by Friday, so we had better decide today, or we lose the deal -- notice that "had better" carries a warning and points to now, not the past. My recommendation is the pilot. Whatever we choose, let us reach a consensus before the call. After should and had better, we use the base verb with no "to".

1. Why does the text use "had better decide today"?
A Because there is a real consequence (the deadline), so it is strong advice with a warning.
B Because the decision happened in the past.
C Because it is softer than "should".
2. What is true about "should" and "ought to" here?
A They have opposite meanings.
B They mean the same; ought to is just a little more formal.
C Should is more formal than ought to.
3. What comes after "should" and "had better"?
A The base verb, with no "to" (should decide, had better warn).
B "to" + verb (should to decide).
C The verb in the past tense.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Should / Ought To / Had Better

GRAMMAR

Para dar conselho há dois níveis. Para conselho normal, use SHOULD ou OUGHT TO (mesmo sentido; ought to é um pouco mais formal). Para conselho forte, com uma consequência real, use HAD BETTER (carrega um aviso). Depois de should e had better, use o verbo base SEM "to"; só ought mantém o "to". Had better NÃO é passado -- aponta para agora e o futuro próximo. / For advice there are two levels. For normal advice use should or ought to (same meaning; ought to is more formal). For strong advice with a real consequence use had better (it carries a warning). After should and had better, use the base verb with no "to"; only ought keeps the "to". Had better is not past -- it points to now and the near future.

FormUseExample
should + base verbnormal advice"we should review the numbers"
ought to + base verbsame, more formal (keep to)"we ought to consider it"
had better + base verbstrong advice + consequence"we had better decide, or..."
Negativesshould not / ought not to / had better not"we had better not delay"

Key point / Ponto-chave: use should/ought to para conselho normal e had better só quando há consequência (aviso). Não use "to" depois de should e had better: "we should to decide" → "we should decide". / Use should/ought to for normal advice and had better only when there is a consequence. No "to" after should and had better.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We weigh the options before we decide."
"We ought consider the safer alternative."
"We had better today, or we lose the contract."
"We had better commit the full budget at once."
"Each path has its pros and ."
"Let us reach a before the call."

Stage 2: Lead a Decision in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of leading a decision in the correct order.

?You weigh the pros and cons of each one: the upside, the downside, the trade-off.
?You state the decision clearly, so everyone knows what to resolve.
?You invite the team to reach a consensus and confirm the next step.
?You lay out the options on the table: two or three realistic alternatives.
?You make a recommendation with should or ought to, and flag any warning with had better.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We should weigh the options before we decide.
Devemos ponderar as opções antes de decidir.
We ought to consider the safer alternative.
Deveríamos considerar a alternativa mais segura.
We had better decide today, or we lose the contract.
É melhor decidirmos hoje, ou perdemos o contrato.
Let us reach a consensus before the call.
Vamos chegar a um consenso antes da ligação.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

There is a deadline on Friday or you lose the deal. Which is best?
A "We should maybe decide sometime."
B "We had better decide today, or we lose the deal."
C "We had better decided today."
You want to give normal, slightly formal advice. Which is best?
A "We ought to consider the alternative."
B "We ought consider the alternative."
C "We ought to considering the alternative."
You want to recommend comparing the choices. Which is best?
A "We should to weigh the options."
B "We should weigh the options and list the pros and cons."
C "We weigh better the options."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real decision your holding faces. Describe it in four sentences: name two options with their pros and cons, recommend one with should or ought to, add one strong warning with had better, and invite your team to reach a consensus.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1We should weigh the options before we decide.Devemos ponderar as opções antes de decidir.
2We ought to consider the safer alternative.Deveríamos considerar a alternativa mais segura.
3We had better decide today, or we lose the contract.É melhor decidirmos hoje, ou perdemos o contrato.
4Each option has its pros and cons, and a clear trade-off.Cada opção tem seus prós e contras, e uma compensação clara.
5Let us reach a consensus before the call.Vamos chegar a um consenso antes da ligação.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Data-Driven -- Describing Trends & Numbers

Read a chart like an analyst. Compare figures with comparatives (Q3 was higher than Q2) and superlatives (the highest figure of the year), show proportion with as ... as (twice as large as), tune the size with degree modifiers (significantly, considerably, slightly, marginally), and report every number in the passive voice (the target was exceeded; the figures were reported by finance). New vocabulary: benchmark, metric, figure, ratio, breakdown, segment, outlier, discrepancy, marginal, substantial, offset, threshold, baseline, variance.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Benchmark -- a standard or reference point used to compare performance
"Q3 revenue was measured against the industry benchmark." (referência / parâmetro)
Metric -- a number used to measure and track performance
"Customer retention is the metric we watch most closely." (métrica / indicador)
Figure -- a specific number that appears in a report or chart
"The sales figure for July was the highest of the year." (cifra / número)
Ratio -- the relationship between two numbers, shown as a proportion
"The cost-to-income ratio is lower than last year." (índice / proporção)
Breakdown -- a detailed split of a total into its separate parts
"The breakdown by region shows Asia growing the fastest." (detalhamento / discriminação)
Segment -- a distinct part of a market, business or set of data
"The premium segment is considerably more profitable." (segmento)
Outlier -- a value that is far higher or lower than all the others
"March was an outlier, far above every other month." (ponto fora da curva / valor atípico)
Discrepancy -- a difference between two figures that are expected to match
"There is a discrepancy between the forecast and the actual figure." (discrepância / divergência)
Marginal -- very small in size or effect; only just noticeable
"The change was marginal, only slightly above last quarter." (marginal / mínimo)
Substantial -- large and significant; clearly important in size
"Revenue showed a substantial increase, significantly higher than Q2." (substancial / considerável)
Offset -- to balance or cancel out one amount with an opposite one
"Higher costs were offset by stronger sales." (compensar / contrabalançar)
Threshold -- a level that must be reached before something takes effect
"Profit passed the threshold set by the board." (limiar / patamar)
Baseline -- the starting figure used as a basis for comparison
"Every result is compared against the January baseline." (linha de base / referência inicial)
Variance -- the difference between a planned figure and the actual one
"The variance between budget and actual was minimal." (variância / desvio)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Benchmark
Metric
Figure
Ratio
Breakdown
Segment
Outlier
Discrepancy
Marginal
Substantial
Offset
Threshold
Baseline
Variance

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the board meeting, I read every figure on the quarterly dashboard. Q3 was the highest figure of the year, significantly higher than Q2 and well above the benchmark; the target was exceeded by a substantial margin. The breakdown by segment showed the premium one growing twice as fast as the standard one, and March stood out as an outlier. Q4 was marginally lower than Q3, but higher costs were offset by stronger sales, so it stayed above the threshold. The variance against the Q1 baseline was minimal, and all figures were reported by finance.

1. How is Q3 compared with Q2?
A "Q3 was significantly higher than Q2" -- comparative + than.
B "Q3 was more higher as Q2."
C "Q3 was the higher than Q2."
2. How is the top figure of the year described?
A "the most highest figure of the year."
B "the highest figure of the year" -- superlative with "the".
C "a higher figure of the year."
3. How is the target reported -- "the target ___"?
A "was exceeded" -- passive: be + past participle.
B "exceeded" -- with no "was".
C "is exceed".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives, Superlatives & the Passive

GRAMMAR

Para descrever dados, você compara números. Comparativo: adjetivo curto + -er + than ("higher than"), adjetivo longo + more ... than ("more profitable than"). Superlativo: the + -est ("the highest of the year") ou the most + adjetivo. Proporção: (twice / half) as + adjetivo + as ("twice as large as"). Use um modificador de grau antes do comparativo para dizer o tamanho da diferença: significantly > considerably > slightly > marginally. E relate cada número na voz passiva: be + partícipio ("the target was exceeded", "the figures were reported by finance"). / Comparatives take "than", superlatives take "the", and the passive reports the number.

Form / functionPatternExample
comparative-er / more ... than"Q3 was higher than Q2"
superlativethe -est / the most ..."the highest figure of the year"
proportion(twice) as ... as"twice as large as the budget"
report the number (passive)be + past participle"the target was exceeded"

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca dobre o comparativo ("more higher" está errado, diga "higher") e use sempre than, nunca "as" ("higher than", não "higher as"). O superlativo leva the ("the highest"). E a passiva SEMPRE precisa do be: "the target was exceeded", "the figures were reported", nunca "the target exceeded". / Never double a comparative, always use "than", and the passive always needs "be".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Q3 was significantly than Q2."
"March was the figure of the year."
"The premium segment is twice as as the standard one."
"The target was by a substantial margin."
"The figures were by finance."
"March was an , far above every other month."

Stage 2: Order the Reporting Steps

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of reading and presenting a results report in the correct order.

?Next, you look at the breakdown by segment and spot any outlier or discrepancy.
?The raw figures arrive from finance, and you check them against the baseline.
?Finally, the dashboard is prepared, and the numbers are presented to the board.
?Then you compare the quarters: which figure is the highest, and how much higher Q3 is than Q2.
?After that, you check whether higher costs were offset and whether the result passed the threshold.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Q3 was significantly higher than Q2.
O terceiro trimestre foi significativamente maior que o segundo.
March was the highest figure of the year.
Março foi a maior cifra do ano.
The target was exceeded by a substantial margin.
A meta foi superada por uma margem substancial.
The figures were reported by finance.
Os números foram reportados pelo financeiro.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to compare two quarters with a degree modifier. Which is best?
A "Q3 was significantly higher than Q2."
B "Q3 was more higher as Q2."
C "Q3 was the higher than Q2."
You want to report a number in the passive, the way a report does. How?
A "The target exceeded by a substantial margin."
B "The target was exceeded by a substantial margin."
C "The target was exceed by a substantial margin."
You want to pick out the top month of the year. Which fits?
A "March was the highest figure of the year."
B "March was the most highest figure of the year."
C "March was a higher figure of the year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting a recent set of results in your business. Compare two figures with a comparative (for example, "Q3 was higher than Q2"), pick out the top one with a superlative (for example, "the highest figure of the year"), and report at least one number in the passive voice (for example, "the target was exceeded"). Use at least two of the new words (benchmark, metric, figure, ratio, breakdown, segment, outlier, discrepancy, marginal, substantial, offset, threshold, baseline, variance...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Q3 was significantly higher than Q2.O terceiro trimestre foi significativamente maior que o segundo.
2March was the highest figure of the year.Março foi a maior cifra do ano.
3The premium segment is twice as large as the standard one.O segmento premium é duas vezes maior que o padrão.
4The target was exceeded by a substantial margin.A meta foi superada por uma margem substancial.
5All figures were reported by finance and are presented in the chart.Todos os números foram reportados pelo financeiro e são apresentados no gráfico.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Contracts & Agreements -- Legal English

Read and discuss a contract like a lawyer. Use the legal modals shall for obligations (each party shall deliver the goods), must for requirements (the warranty must cover twelve months), and may for permissions (either party may terminate), with shall not / must not / may not for prohibitions, and keep the passive voice for every term (the goods shall be delivered; confidential information must not be disclosed). New vocabulary: indemnity, counterparty, governing law, jurisdiction, remedy, default, covenant, stipulate, confidentiality, force majeure, hereby, in good faith.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Indemnity -- a contractual promise to compensate the other party for a loss
"Under the indemnity clause, the supplier shall cover any third-party claim." (indenização / ressarcimento)
Counterparty -- the other party to a contract, the side you sign with
"As the counterparty, we shall deliver within thirty days." (contraparte / a outra parte)
Governing law -- the body of law that controls and interprets the contract
"The governing law is the law of New York." (lei aplicável / legislação regente)
Jurisdiction -- the place whose courts have legal authority over a dispute
"Any dispute shall be settled in the courts of that jurisdiction." (jurisdição / foro)
Remedy -- the legal solution available when a contract term is broken
"If the supplier fails, the buyer has a clear remedy." (remédio jurídico / reparação)
Default -- a failure to meet an obligation set out in the contract
"If delivery is prevented, neither party shall be in default." (inadimplemento / descumprimento)
Covenant -- a formal, binding promise to do or not to do something
"Each side accepts a covenant to act in good faith." (pacto / compromisso formal)
Stipulate -- to state a condition or requirement formally in a contract
"The agreement stipulates that the warranty must cover twelve months." (estipular / determinar)
Confidentiality -- the duty to keep shared information private and undisclosed
"Confidentiality is essential: the data must not be disclosed." (confidencialidade / sigilo)
Force majeure -- unforeseeable events beyond control that excuse a party from performing
"Under the force majeure clause, neither party is in default." (força maior / caso fortuito)
Hereby -- by means of this document or statement (a formal, legal word)
"The parties hereby agree to the terms of this agreement." (pela presente / por este meio)
In good faith -- honestly, fairly and with sincere intention to keep the agreement
"Each party shall act in good faith at all times." (de boa-fé)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Indemnity
Counterparty
Governing law
Jurisdiction
Remedy
Default
Covenant
Stipulate
Confidentiality
Force majeure
Hereby
In good faith

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before I sign, I read every clause of the Supply Agreement with my lawyer. The obligations use shall: each party shall deliver the goods within thirty days, and the counterparty shall act in good faith. The requirements use must: the agreement stipulates that the warranty must cover twelve months, and, for confidentiality, the data must not be disclosed. The permissions use may: either party may terminate with sixty days notice, and either party may request an audit. The governing law is the law of New York, and any dispute shall be settled in that jurisdiction. There is a force majeure clause too, so if delivery is prevented, neither party is in default and there is a clear remedy. The parties hereby agree to the terms.

1. How is the delivery obligation stated?
A "Each party shall deliver the goods" -- shall + base verb (obligation).
B "Each party shall to deliver the goods."
C "Each party may deliver the goods."
2. Why does "either party may terminate" use "may"?
A Because it is a firm duty.
B Because it is a permission -- a right, not an obligation.
C Because it is a prohibition.
3. How is the confidentiality term written -- "the data ___"?
A "must not be disclosed" -- passive: modal + be + past participle.
B "must not disclosed" -- with no "be".
C "may not disclose".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Shall, Must & May + the Passive

GRAMMAR

Em inglês jurídico, cada tipo de regra tem seu modal. Shall + verbo base = obrigação ("each party shall deliver"). Must + verbo base = requisito ("the warranty must cover twelve months"). May + verbo base = permissão ("either party may terminate"). Shall not / must not / may not = proibição. Sempre MODAL + verbo na BASE, nunca "shall to deliver". E para escrever o termo em si, use a voz passiva: modal + be + partícipio ("the goods shall be delivered", "the data must not be disclosed"). / Shall = obligation, must = requirement, may = permission, and the passive for every term.

Modal / functionFormExample
shall (obligation)shall + base verb"each party shall deliver the goods"
must (requirement)must + base verb"the warranty must cover twelve months"
may (permission)may + base verb"either party may terminate"
Write the term (passive)modal + be + past participle"the goods shall be delivered"

Key point / Ponto-chave: o modal carrega a regra, e a passiva mantém o foco no termo. A passiva SEMPRE precisa do BE: "the goods shall be delivered", "the data must not be disclosed", nunca "shall delivered" / "must not disclosed". E o modal sempre vem com o verbo na base: "shall deliver", nunca "shall to deliver". / The passive always needs "be", and a modal always takes the base verb.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Each party shall the goods within thirty days."
"The warranty must twelve months."
"Either party may the agreement with notice."
"The goods shall be within thirty days."
"Confidential information must not be ."
"The agreement that the payment must be made on time."

Stage 2: Order the Review Steps

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of reviewing and signing a contract in the correct order.

?Next, you check the permissions: what either party may do, and how the contract may be terminated.
?The draft agreement arrives, and you read every clause carefully with your lawyer.
?Finally, the agreement is signed, the obligations take effect, and the goods shall be delivered as the contract stipulates.
?Then you check the obligations: what each party shall do, and what must happen on time.
?After that, you negotiate the points you disagree with, in good faith, until both sides accept the terms.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Each party shall deliver the goods within thirty days.
Cada parte deverá entregar as mercadorias em até trinta dias.
The warranty must cover twelve months.
A garantia deve cobrir doze meses.
Either party may terminate the agreement with sixty days notice.
Qualquer das partes pode rescindir o contrato com sessenta dias de aviso.
Confidential information must not be disclosed.
Informações confidenciais não devem ser divulgadas.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to state a firm obligation to deliver. Which is best?
A "Each party shall deliver the goods within thirty days."
B "Each party shall to deliver the goods within thirty days."
C "Each party may deliver the goods within thirty days."
You want to state a term in the passive, the way a contract does. How?
A "The goods shall delivered within thirty days."
B "The goods shall be delivered within thirty days."
C "The goods shall delivering within thirty days."
You want to express a permission, not a duty. Which fits?
A "Either party may request an audit once a year."
B "Either party shall request an audit once a year."
C "Either party must to request an audit once a year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are walking a new partner through a contract in your business. State one obligation with "shall" (for example, "each party shall deliver the goods"), one requirement with "must" (for example, "the warranty must cover twelve months"), and one permission with "may" (for example, "either party may terminate"). Put at least one term in the passive voice (for example, "the goods shall be delivered"). Use at least two of the new words (indemnity, counterparty, governing law, jurisdiction, remedy, default, covenant, stipulate, confidentiality, force majeure, hereby, in good faith...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Each party shall deliver the goods within thirty days.Cada parte deverá entregar as mercadorias em até trinta dias.
2The warranty must cover twelve months.A garantia deve cobrir doze meses.
3Either party may terminate the agreement with sixty days notice.Qualquer das partes pode rescindir o contrato com sessenta dias de aviso.
4Confidential information must not be disclosed.Informações confidenciais não devem ser divulgadas.
5The goods shall be delivered exactly as the contract stipulates.As mercadorias deverão ser entregues exatamente como o contrato estipula.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results

Tell your professional story like a seasoned executive. Use the present perfect for your experience and the results that still matter today (I have managed this holding for over ten years; we have closed three deals this quarter), the simple past for finished, dated milestones (I led the restructuring in 2019; we opened the office last March), and the passive voice for results (the target was exceeded; the groundwork has been laid). New vocabulary: spearhead, turnaround, seasoned, proven, instrumental, hands-on, well-versed, longstanding, surpass, tenure, yield, groundwork.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Seasoned -- highly experienced and capable after years of practice
"She is a seasoned executive with over a decade at the top." (experiente / calejado)
Proven -- demonstrated to work or to be reliable over time
"We have a proven model for growing the holding." (comprovado)
Spearhead -- to lead an important project or initiative from the front
"She spearheaded the recovery of the family business." (liderar / encabeçar)
Turnaround -- a recovery that moves a business from difficulty to success
"The turnaround we started two years ago has paid off." (recuperação / virada)
Instrumental -- playing a key, decisive role in making something happen
"She was instrumental in closing the supplier deal." (fundamental / decisivo)
Hands-on -- directly involved in the practical, day-to-day work
"I take a hands-on approach with the accounting team." (prático / mão na massa)
Well-versed -- very knowledgeable and skilled in a particular area
"She is well-versed in fiscal and tax matters." (versado / conhecedor)
Longstanding -- having existed or continued for a long time
"We have a longstanding relationship with our US partners." (de longa data)
Surpass -- to go beyond or do better than a goal or expectation
"The results have surpassed every target this year." (superar / ultrapassar)
Tenure -- the period during which someone holds a role or position
"Sales have doubled during her tenure as partner." (mandato / período no cargo)
Yield -- to produce or generate something, such as results or returns
"The new strategy has yielded strong results." (render / gerar)
Groundwork -- the preparatory work that makes later success possible
"The groundwork has been laid for next year." (base / alicerce)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Seasoned
Proven
Spearhead
Turnaround
Instrumental
Hands-on
Well-versed
Longstanding
Surpass
Tenure
Yield
Groundwork

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the annual review, I prepare my story. I have managed this holding for over ten years, and I am a seasoned, hands-on manager, well-versed in the fiscal side. The turnaround I spearheaded two years ago has yielded a proven result: this has been our strongest year. We have closed three major contracts this quarter, and sales have grown since January. For the dated milestones, I switch to the simple past: I led the restructuring in 2019, we opened the Pittsburgh office last March, and we signed the supplier agreement in June. And I keep the passive for the results: every target has been surpassed, and the groundwork has been laid for next year. It is the strongest year of my tenure.

1. How does the writer describe her experience?
A "I have managed this holding for over ten years" -- present perfect, open period.
B "I managed this holding in over ten years."
C "I am managing this holding since ten years."
2. Why is "I led the restructuring in 2019" in the simple past?
A Because it is still happening now.
B Because it is a finished event at a definite time ("in 2019").
C Because the doer is unknown.
3. How is the result reported -- "every target ___"?
A "has been surpassed" -- passive: has been + past participle.
B "surpassed" -- with no "be".
C "is surpassing".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Simple Past & the Passive

GRAMMAR

Use o present perfect (have / has + partícipio) para experiência e resultados num período ainda ABERTO ("I have managed it for ten years", "we have closed three deals this quarter") -- sinais: for, since, this quarter, ever, already, so far. Use o simple past (verbo + -ed / irregular) para eventos TERMINADOS com tempo definido ("I led the restructuring in 2019", "we opened the office last March") -- sinais: in 2019, last March, ago, yesterday. Para relatar um resultado, use a voz passiva (be / was / have been + partícipio): "the target was exceeded", "the groundwork has been laid". / Present perfect for an open period, simple past for a finished time, the passive for a result.

Tense / functionFormExample
Present perfect (experience/result)have / has + past participle"I have managed it for ten years"
Simple past (finished, dated)verb + -ed / irregular"I led the restructuring in 2019"
Signal wordsfor / since vs in 2019 / last March"sales have grown since January"
Report a result (passive)be / was / have been + participle"the target was exceeded"

Key point / Ponto-chave: no momento em que você adiciona um tempo definido (in 2019, last March), troque para o simple past -- "I led it in 2019", nunca "I have led it in 2019". E a passiva SEMPRE precisa do BE: "the target was exceeded", "the groundwork has been laid", nunca "the target exceeded". / A definite past time takes the simple past, and the passive always needs "be".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have this holding for over ten years."
"I the restructuring in 2019."
"Sales have since January."
"The target was last year."
"The groundwork has been for next year."
"She the turnaround of the business."

Stage 2: Order the Career Story

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building and telling a track record in the correct order.

?When a difficult moment comes, you spearhead a turnaround and the business recovers.
?You join the company and learn it from the inside, doing the hands-on work and building your experience.
?Finally, you tell the story: experience in the present perfect, milestones in the past, the passive for the results.
?Over the years, you lead projects and the results add up: targets are met and the groundwork is laid.
?The dated milestones pile up: a deal is signed, an office is opened, a record is set.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I have managed this holding for over ten years.
Eu administro este grupo há mais de dez anos.
I led the restructuring in 2019.
Eu liderei a reestruturação em 2019.
Every target has been surpassed this year.
Cada meta foi superada este ano.
The groundwork has been laid for next year.
A base foi preparada para o próximo ano.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to say how long you have run the business (and you still do). Which is best?
A "I have managed this holding for over ten years."
B "I manage this holding since ten years."
C "I managed this holding for over ten years ago."
You report a project finished in a specific year. How do you say it?
A "I have led the restructuring in 2019."
B "I led the restructuring in 2019."
C "I am leading the restructuring in 2019."
You want to report this year's result, focusing on the result. Which fits?
A "Every target has been surpassed this year."
B "Every target surpassed this year."
C "Every target is surpass this year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting your professional story to a new partner. Describe your experience using the present perfect (for example, "I have managed this holding for over ten years"), report one finished milestone in the simple past (for example, "I led the restructuring in 2019"), and report one result in the passive voice (for example, "every target has been surpassed"). Use at least two of the new words (seasoned, spearhead, turnaround, instrumental, hands-on, well-versed, surpass, yield, groundwork...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I have managed this holding for over ten years.Eu administro este grupo há mais de dez anos.
2We have closed three major contracts this quarter.Fechamos três grandes contratos neste trimestre.
3I led the restructuring in 2019.Eu liderei a reestruturação em 2019.
4Every target has been surpassed this year.Cada meta foi superada este ano.
5The groundwork has been laid for next year.A base foi preparada para o próximo ano.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

The Art Market -- Trends, Artists & Value

Talk about the art market like a collector: read the trends, compare artists and value, and report a sale. Use superlatives (the most sought-after artist, the highest price ever paid) and trend verbs (prices have soared, the market is booming) to describe the market, and report results in the passive voice (the painting was sold for..., the work has been appraised at...). New vocabulary: auction, bid, lot, appraisal, valuation, provenance, authenticate, masterpiece, sought-after, emerging, blue-chip, speculative.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Auction -- a public sale where a work goes to the highest offer
"The painting will come up at the evening auction." (leilão)
Bid -- an offer of a price for a work at auction
"Set your top bid before you walk into the room." (lance / dar um lance)
Lot -- a single numbered item, or set, offered for sale
"Lot forty-seven is the painting I would watch." (lote)
Appraisal -- a professional opinion of how much a work is worth
"The appraisal came in at three hundred thousand dollars." (avaliação / laudo)
Valuation -- the assessed monetary worth of a work
"A smart purchase is one where the valuation supports the price." (valor estimado)
Provenance -- the documented record of every owner a work has had
"The provenance is clean, so there are no questions about its history." (procedência)
Authenticate -- to prove a work is genuine and by the named artist
"The work has been authenticated by the artist's estate." (autenticar)
Masterpiece -- an outstanding work, often an artist's very best
"The masterpiece in lot twelve is the most expensive piece of the night." (obra-prima)
Sought-after -- in high demand because many collectors want it
"She is one of the most sought-after artists on the market." (cobiçado / muito procurado)
Emerging -- new and starting to gain recognition (an emerging artist)
"It is an early painting by an emerging artist." (emergente)
Blue-chip -- established, safe and high in value, with a strong track record
"This is a strong, blue-chip name in the making." (consagrado / de primeira linha)
Speculative -- risky -- bought hoping the value will rise, with no guarantee
"An emerging artist is more speculative than a blue-chip one." (especulativo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Auction
Bid
Lot
Appraisal
Valuation
Provenance
Authenticate
Masterpiece
Sought-after
Emerging
Blue-chip
Speculative

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Tonight I am at the evening auction, watching lot forty-seven. The artist is emerging, but she is already one of the most sought-after names in the room. Prices for her work have soared, and contemporary art is the fastest-growing segment on the market -- it is booming. Before I bid, I check the provenance: the work has been authenticated and it was acquired from the studio, so the history is clean. The appraisal, or valuation, is around three hundred thousand dollars, and a similar piece was sold last month for almost double. The name is still a little speculative, not blue-chip yet, so I set my top bid in advance and I will not chase it. The most expensive masterpiece in the room is not always the best buy.

1. How does the writer rank the artist?
A "one of the most sought-after names" -- a superlative.
B "a quite wanted artist."
C "the most wantedest name."
2. Which phrase describes the movement of prices?
A "prices are sold" -- a passive.
B "prices have soared" -- a trend verb.
C "prices are the cheapest."
3. How is the sale reported -- "a similar piece ___ last month"?
A "was sold" -- passive: was + past participle.
B "sold" -- with no "be".
C "is selling".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Superlatives, Trend Verbs & the Passive

GRAMMAR

Para comparar e ranquear, use superlativos: adjetivo curto = THE + adjetivo + -EST ("the highest price"); adjetivo longo = THE MOST + adjetivo ("the most sought-after artist"). Para descrever o movimento do mercado, use verbos de tendência: soar / climb / rise / boom (subir). Para relatar um resultado, use a voz passiva (be / was / have been + partícipio): "the painting was sold", "the work has been appraised". / Use superlatives to rank, trend verbs to describe movement, and the passive to report a result.

FunctionFormExample
Superlative (short)the + adj + -est"the highest price ever paid"
Superlative (long)the most + adjective"the most sought-after artist"
Trend verbsoar / climb / rise / boom"prices have soared"
Report a resultbe / was / have been + participle"the painting was sold"

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca junte os dois superlativos ("the most highest" está errado -- é "the highest"). E a passiva SEMPRE precisa do BE: "the work WAS appraised", "the painting HAS BEEN sold", nunca "the work appraised". / Never double the superlative, and the passive always needs "be" (was / were / been).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Prices for emerging artists have this year."
"That was the price ever paid for the artist."
"The painting was for a record price."
"The work has been at three hundred thousand."
"Before you buy, check the ."
"Set your top before the sale."

Stage 2: Order the Sale

Order

Listen first, then put the journey of a work to the saleroom in the correct order.

?A specialist gives it a valuation and the auction house prints an estimate in the catalogue.
?The work leaves the artist's studio and its provenance begins: every owner and every sale is recorded.
?The result is reported: the painting was sold for a record price, and the new owner is added to its provenance.
?Before it can be sold, the piece is authenticated, so that buyers know it is genuine.
?On the night of the sale, collectors place their bids, and the lot is sold to the highest bidder.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

She is one of the most sought-after artists on the market.
Ela é uma das artistas mais cobiçadas do mercado.
Prices have soared and the market is booming.
Os preços dispararam e o mercado está em alta.
I set my top bid in advance because the valuation supports it.
Eu defino meu lance máximo com antecedência porque a avaliação o sustenta.
The painting was sold for the highest price of the night.
O quadro foi vendido pelo maior preço da noite.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to say an artist is number one in demand. Which is best?
A "She is the most sought-after artist of her generation."
B "She is the most sought-afterest artist."
C "She is very wanted artist."
A painting sold last night. How do you report it naturally?
A "The painting sold by the house."
B "The painting was sold for a record price."
C "The painting is selling now."
You want to reassure a buyer that the work is genuine. Which fits?
A "The work has been authenticated and the provenance is clean."
B "The work authenticated already."
C "The work is authenticate."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at an evening auction, watching a lot you love. Describe the artist and the work using a superlative (for example, "one of the most sought-after artists"), describe the market with a trend verb (for example, "prices have soared"), and report a recent sale of a similar piece in the passive voice (for example, "a similar piece was sold last month"). Then say whether you would bid, and why.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1She is one of the most sought-after artists on the market.Ela é uma das artistas mais cobiçadas do mercado.
2Prices have soared and the market is booming.Os preços dispararam e o mercado está em alta.
3The provenance is clean and the work has been authenticated.A procedência é limpa e a obra foi autenticada.
4I set my top bid in advance because the valuation supports it.Eu defino meu lance máximo com antecedência porque a avaliação o sustenta.
5The painting was sold for the highest price of the night.O quadro foi vendido pelo maior preço da noite.

Aulas Disponíveis

01
Who Is Maria Claudia? — Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait
Present simple + continuous -- 40 slides
02
The Holding Structure
Describing Your Business Empire — 40 slides
03
Running the Meeting
Opening, Agenda, Action Items — 30 slides
04
Money Talks
Financial Vocabulary for Executives — 31 slides
05
Colors and Opinions
Describing Art, Expressing Preferences — 31 slides
06
The Passive Voice
Reports Are Written, Paintings Are Exhibited — 31 slides
07
Negotiation 101
Prices, Terms, Conditions — 31 slides
08
Strategic Proposals -- What If?
Second conditional + hypothetical proposals -- 30 slides
09
Gallery Night -- Socializing About Art & Business
Opinion & stance toolkit + networking at a gallery -- 30 slides
10
Review & Integration -- Block 1 Checkpoint
Consolidate aulas 1-9 + summary & linking language -- 30 slides
11
Conference Calls -- Managing Tech & Time Zones
Polite requests on a video call + handling tech and time zones -- 30 slides
12
Fiscal and Tax Talk -- Communicating With Accountants
Talking to your accountants about deadlines, audits and compliance -- present perfect with for/since -- 30 slides
13
Making Proposals -- Pitching Ideas to the Board
Pitch an idea to your partners: rationale, scope, a pilot and a clear value proposition -- proposing structures (I would like to suggest..., Why don't we...?, What if we...?) -- 30 slides
14
The Art Market -- Trends, Artists & Value
Talk about the art market like a collector: read the trends, compare artists and value, and report a sale -- superlatives (the most sought-after artist, the highest price ever paid), trend verbs (prices have soared, the market is booming) and the passive for results (the painting was sold for...) -- 30 slides
15
Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results
Tell your professional story like a seasoned executive: describe your experience and the results that still matter today with the present perfect (I have managed this holding for over ten years; we have closed three deals this quarter), report finished, dated milestones in the simple past (I led the restructuring in 2019), and keep the passive voice for results (the target was exceeded, the groundwork has been laid) -- 30 slides
16
Contracts & Agreements -- Legal English
Read and discuss a contract like a lawyer: state obligations with shall (each party shall deliver the goods), requirements with must (the warranty must cover twelve months), and permissions with may (either party may terminate), use shall not / must not / may not for prohibitions, and keep the passive voice for every term (the goods shall be delivered; confidential information must not be disclosed) -- 30 slides
17
Data-Driven -- Describing Trends & Numbers
Read a chart like an analyst: compare figures with comparatives (Q3 was higher than Q2) and superlatives (the highest figure of the year), show proportion with as ... as (twice as large as), tune the size with degree modifiers (significantly, considerably, slightly, marginally), and report every number in the passive voice (the target was exceeded; the figures were reported by finance) -- 30 slides
18
Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...
Recommend and decide in a meeting: should and ought to for advice, and had better for strong advice with a consequence -- weigh the options, the pros and cons, and reach a consensus -- 27 slides
19
Houston, We Have Culture -- US vs Brazil
Compare business and art culture across the US and Brazil: comparison structures whereas, while, unlike, and prepositions of comparison (similar to, different from) -- punctuality, hierarchy, directness, formality, etiquette, and how to build rapport -- 27 slides
20
The Big Review -- Block 2 Integration
Review and integrate Block 2: bring together present perfect, modals (shall / must / may, should / ought to / had better), trend language, and comparison structures (whereas / unlike / different from) in one full negotiation scenario, then run a self-assessment -- 27 slides

Materiais Complementares

Lesson 1 — Who Is Maria Claudia? — Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait

Podcast
Planet Money — Episode 403: What Can We Do With Our Shell Companies?

Planet Money sets up its own offshore shell companies to figure out how these corporate holding structures actually work and what people use them for.

Listen with headphones and pause to note business terms like 'shell company', 'offshore', and 'holdings' as they appear.

Open episode ↗

TED Talk
Julian Treasure — How to Speak So That People Want to Listen

Essential communication skills for executives. Focus on how he structures his message and uses vocal variety.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and pause to repeat key phrases.

Watch on TED ↗

Filme
Moneyball (2011)

Negotiation, decision-making, and executive English in a real business context. Watch how Billy Beane presents data to stakeholders.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Pay attention to the meeting and negotiation scenes.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Lesson 2 — The Holding Structure — Describing Your Business Empire

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Scaling a Business Beyond the Family Playbook

Jessica Johnson-Cope, CEO of a rare third-generation family firm, explains the governance and strategy that let a family business not just survive but thrive. English business vocabulary about ownership, board, and long-term decisions in real context.

Listen with headphones for words like board, shareholders, and assets.

Open episode ↗

Documentary
Abstract: The Art of Design — Season 1, Episode 1

Netflix. Design thinking connects to your art interest, and the English narration is rich with advanced vocabulary and clear pronunciation.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and pause to repeat sentences you find interesting.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Film
The Big Short (2015)

Financial vocabulary, corporate structures, and American English at its sharpest. Pay attention to how characters explain complex business concepts in simple terms.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on scenes with boardroom discussions and financial negotiations.

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

Lesson 3 — Running the Meeting

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Best of IdeaCast: To Build Stronger Teams, Ask Better Questions

Harvard professors Alison Wood Brooks and Leslie John share techniques for structuring conversations and meetings so every voice is heard, adjusting the frame and tone of your questions to build trust and unlock ideas.

Listen for meeting phrases like 'let's move on to' and 'action items.'

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED — "5 Tips for Dealing with Meeting Overload" (The Way We Work)

Practical advice on making meetings productive. Notice how the speaker uses meeting vocabulary: agenda, action items, wrap up. Connects directly to this lesson.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 phrases you could use in your own meetings.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Succession (HBO) — Season 1, Episode 6: "Which Side Are You On?"

A high-stakes board meeting with corporate language at its peak. Notice how characters open discussions, set agendas, and assign responsibilities. Rich in meeting vocabulary.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on the boardroom scenes and how executives facilitate the meeting.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Lesson 4 — Money Talks

Podcast
Planet Money — Episode 737: 17 Deals In 17 Minutes

A fast-paced Planet Money episode packing 17 short money stories into 17 minutes, including why goods get oddly specific prices like $12.11.

Listen for finance vocabulary such as 'deal', 'price', 'profit', and 'growth' — one new term per story.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED — "How to speak so that people want to listen" by Julian Treasure

A powerful talk (10 min) about communication skills. Notice how Treasure uses comparatives and numbers to make his points. The delivery techniques apply directly to financial presentations.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Focus on how the speaker structures comparisons and uses vocal variety to present data.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Billions (Showtime) — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

High-stakes finance at its best. The pilot episode is packed with financial vocabulary: revenue, profit margins, quarterly results. Pay attention to how characters present numbers and use comparatives in negotiations.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on the scenes where characters discuss financial performance and compare results.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Lesson 5 — Colors and Opinions

Podcast
The Lonely Palette — Episode 16: Vincent Van Gogh's Postman Joseph Roulin (1888)

Host Tamar Avishai zooms in on a single Van Gogh portrait, describing its colors, brushwork, and the artist's emotional connection to his subject. Ideal for hearing descriptive art vocabulary like vibrant, subtle, and composition.

Pause when she describes the painting and repeat the adjectives (vibrant, bold, subtle) out loud.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED — "The Power of Vulnerability" by Brene Brown

A powerful talk (20 min) about expressing opinions and being authentic. Notice how Brown uses "I find it..." and "In my opinion..." structures naturally. The vocabulary of emotions and opinions connects directly with Lesson 5.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down every time the speaker expresses a personal opinion and note the structure she uses.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Abstract: The Art of Design (Netflix) — Season 1, Episode 2: "Tinker Hatfield: Footwear Design"

A visually stunning episode about design, color choices, and composition. Pay attention to how designers describe their work using adjective order: "a beautiful large contemporary American sculpture." Notice expressions of opinion throughout.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on how the designers express their opinions about art and design using "I find it..." and similar structures.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Lesson 6 — The Passive Voice

Podcast
Grammar Girl (Quick and Dirty Tips) — Active Voice Versus Passive Voice

Mignon Fogarty explains the difference between active and passive voice with clear examples ('Steve loves Amy' vs. 'Amy is loved by Steve') and when passive is actually the better choice, including crime reports and scientific writing.

After listening, rewrite three passive sentences from your own work into active voice.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED-Ed — "How to use rhetoric to get what you want"

A short animated lesson (5 min) about persuasive language. Notice how passive structures are used in formal rhetoric: "decisions are made," "arguments are presented." The passive voice creates authority and distance in professional communication.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Count how many passive voice sentences you can identify in the narration.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Crown (Netflix) — Season 1, Episode 1: "Wolferton Splash"

Formal English in a royal and government context. Pay attention to how passive voice is used in official communication: "The King is informed," "Decisions are taken by the Cabinet," "The ceremony is conducted." This episode is rich with passive structures in formal, professional English.

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Write down every passive sentence you notice and identify the agent (by whom?).

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Lesson 7 — Negotiation 101

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 557: Did a New Job Get Off to a Rocky Start? How to Talk About It
