A short professional episode on how to talk about your job and your early experience in a new role in natural English. Good practice for describing what you do and how work is going, using present and past simple.

After listening, describe your own job and first week in 4-5 sentences.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Planet Money — A Secret Meeting And The Birth Of The Federal Reserve

NPR's Planet Money tells the story of the secret 1910 meeting that created the U.S. Federal Reserve — clear, conversational American English about money and finance, on a theme close to the original shell-companies episode.

Assista com legendas em ingles e anote 3 termos de economia; depois tente resumir o video em voz alta em uma frase.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Shark Tank — Season 1, Episode 1

Real negotiation vocabulary in action. Entrepreneurs pitch their business ideas and negotiate deals with investors. Pay attention to how they discuss prices, terms, counteroffers, and concessions. Notice the use of conditional language: "If you invest..., I will give you..."

Tip: watch in English with English subtitles. Focus on how each side uses leverage and makes concessions to reach an agreement.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Lesson 8 — Strategic Proposals -- What If?

Series
"Succession" -- boardroom strategy scenes

Executives weigh hypothetical acquisitions and expansions in almost every episode. Listen for how they propose options ("If we sold the division...") and argue about the upside, the downside, and the trade-off before deciding.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases no segundo condicional (if + past simple, would/could + verbo) e duas palavras de proposta (scenario, trade-off, upside, downside).

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Make Better Decisions, Think Like a Venture Capitalist

Stanford's Ilya Strebulaev shows how VCs frame uncertain scenarios, operationalize risk, allocate resources, and embrace disagreement over easy consensus. Great input to review feasibility, hypotheticals, and strategic bets.

Notice modal verbs (would, could, might) used to weigh options and risk.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
The second conditional - 6 Minute Grammar

Clear, short explainer on the second conditional for hypothetical and unlikely situations, including "If I were you...". Watch how the if-clause stays in the past simple while the result uses would, could, or might.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e reformule uma decisão real do seu trabalho como hipótese: "If we acquired the plant, we would double our capacity."

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Gallery Night -- Socializing About Art & Business

Series
"Emily in Paris" -- gallery openings & party scenes

The show is full of vernissages, launch parties, and social mixers where characters mingle, break the ice, and share quick opinions about art and brands. Great input for networking small talk and reacting to what you see.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três formas diferentes de dar uma opinião (não só "I think") e duas frases de socialização (mingle, small talk, break the ice).

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
The Art Newspaper — The Week in Art: Frida Kahlo at Tate Modern, the Brexit effect, a Renaissance tarot deck

Host Ben Luke tours the Frida Kahlo exhibition at Tate Modern, discusses Brexit's decade-long impact on the art trade with an Institute of Art and Law expert, and explores the Visconti-Sforza Tarot at the Morgan Library. Curators and critics give candid impressions of the work.

Note how guests give opinions on exhibitions (impressive, striking, disappointing) and reuse two phrases.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
How to Express Your Opinion in English

A short, clear guide to phrases for giving opinions at different strengths -- confident, neutral, and tentative -- and for agreeing and disagreeing politely. It also covers the classic mistakes "according to me" and "I am agree".

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma opinião de três formas -- forte, neutra e hesitante: "I'd argue that... / From my perspective... / I'm inclined to think...".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Review & Integration -- Block 1 Checkpoint

Series
"Succession" -- boardroom recaps & summaries

Executives constantly recap deals, summarize quarters, and deliver the bottom line under pressure. Listen for how they connect points with linking words and report results in the passive ("the deal was signed", "the offer was rejected").

Dica: assista uma cena de reunião com legenda em inglês e anote três frases de resumo (to sum up, on the whole, the bottom line) e dois exemplos de voz passiva.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — 10 Gems from IdeaCast's First 1,000 Episodes

The hosts recap and summarize the sharpest insights on strategy, decisions, and leadership from a thousand interviews with executives. A whole episode built on summarizing and concluding — perfect input to review this lesson's phrases.

Collect the recap phrases they use ('the big takeaway', 'to sum up', 'in short').

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Present and past passives - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear guides to connecting ideas (firstly, moreover, however, in conclusion) and to forming the passive voice (be + past participle). It covers the classic mistakes "was wrote" and "was sended" -- exactly the review focus of this lesson.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e reformule uma frase ativa como passiva: "the team wrote the report" → "the report was written by the team".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Conference Calls -- Managing Tech & Time Zones

Series
"The Office" (US) -- the video conference episodes

The branch is full of clumsy video calls, frozen screens, and people talking over each other on mute -- a comic but realistic window into conference-call English. Listen for how characters interrupt, ask each other to repeat, and handle the tech.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três pedidos educados (Could you... / Would you mind...) e duas frases sobre problemas técnicos (cut out, freeze, on mute).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 552: How to Continue Small Talk at the Start of a Work Call

A clear, practical episode on the exact language of remote work calls: opening the call, making small talk, and easing into the conversation before business starts. Perfect input to review this lesson's polite call phrases.

Note the opening phrases and try them at the start of your next video call.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
How to be polite in English: Ask BBC Learning English

Short, clear guides to polite requests ("Could you...?", "Would you mind + -ing") and to the passive voice (be + past participle). It also flags the classic mistake "would you mind to do" and shows when to use the passive instead of naming who did something.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia de duas formas -- um pedido educado e uma frase na voz passiva sobre uma chamada ("the line was dropped").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Fiscal and Tax Talk -- Communicating With Accountants

Series
"Succession" -- the boardroom and accountant scenes

A family holding, advisors, and constant talk of the books, audits, liabilities and compliance -- exactly your world. Listen for how executives discuss what "has been done" and how long things "have been" a certain way, in fast, natural Business English.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases no present perfect com for/since e duas frases na voz passiva sobre números ou auditoria.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Down to Business English — The Stablecoin Gold Rush: Why Wall Street Is Betting on Digital Dollars | 388

The hosts explain how banks and governments are adopting stablecoins, covering finance vocabulary around digital dollars, regulation and Wall Street's bets.

Pause after each finance term (reserve, regulation, issuer) and say it aloud before continuing.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Present perfect with 'for' and 'since' - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear guides to the present perfect for duration: when to use "for" with a period and "since" with a starting point, and why "I work here since 2015" is wrong. It also reviews the passive voice (be + past participle) used so often in finance.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre o seu trabalho -- uma frase com "for", uma com "since", e uma na voz passiva sobre a declaração.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Making Proposals -- Pitching Ideas to the Board

Series
"Shark Tank" / "Dragons' Den" -- the pitch scenes

Founders pitch ideas to investors in minutes: rationale first, then scope, numbers and a value proposition, while the panel pushes back. Exactly the structure you use with the board. Listen for how they open ("What if you...", "I would suggest...") and how deals "are done" and proposals "are accepted".

Dica: assista a um pitch com legenda em inglês e anote três frames de proposta e duas frases na voz passiva sobre o resultado (a deal was made, the offer was accepted).

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — 216. Wired for Words: A Neuroscientist's Guide to Influence

Neuroscientist Emily Falk explains what makes a proposal land in the listener's brain -- relevance, framing, and story -- so your pitch actually moves people to decide.

Listen for why relevance matters, then reframe a proposal you have around what your audience cares about.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
How to make suggestions in English

Short, clear guides to the language of proposing: suggest / recommend / propose, "Why don't we...?" and "What if we...?", plus a review of the passive voice (be + past participle) used to report results -- the exact grammar you need to fix your recurring habit.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre o seu trabalho -- um frame de proposta, e uma frase na voz passiva sobre uma decisão (the plan was approved).

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — The Art Market -- Trends, Artists & Value

Documentary
"The Price of Everything" (HBO) -- inside the art market

A sharp look at how value is created in contemporary art: auctions, dealers, emerging artists and blue-chip names. Listen for how experts rank works with superlatives ("one of the most important...") and report sales in the passive ("the work was sold for...", "the piece has been acquired by..."). Exactly the language of this lesson.

Dica: assista a uma cena de leilão com legenda em inglês e anote dois superlativos e duas frases na voz passiva sobre uma venda (a work was sold for..., a piece was acquired by...).

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
The Art Angle (Artnet News) — Where Art Insiders Are Placing Their Bets in 2026

After a rocky 2025 of gallery closures and cooling demand, Artnet's editors and former Art Basel director Marc Spiegler weigh which market segments are recovering, how the auction weeks performed, and what price trends to watch in 2026. Perfect input to review market vocabulary.

Jot down each market term you hear (segment, lot, hammer price, sought-after) as it comes up.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Comparatives and superlatives - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear guides to two grammar points from this lesson: how to form superlatives (the + -est vs the most + adjective) and how to use the passive voice (be + past participle) to report results -- the exact grammar you need to fix your recurring habit of dropping "be".

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre uma obra de arte -- um superlativo (the most sought-after) e uma frase na voz passiva sobre uma venda (the painting was sold).

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Present Perfect in Business -- Experience & Results

Talk
TED Talk -- a leader telling their career story

Search TED for a CEO or founder telling the story of a business they turned around. Listen for how they describe their experience in the present perfect ("I have spent twenty years building this") and report dated moments in the simple past ("in 2015, we launched..."), plus results in the passive ("the company was saved", "targets have been surpassed"). The exact tense-switching of this lesson.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases no present perfect (experiência/resultado) e duas no simple past (eventos com data).

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz — Airbnb: Joe Gebbia

Co-founder Joe Gebbia tells the full Airbnb story: from renting air mattresses to pay the rent, through the near-failure early years, to building a company with more rooms than the world's biggest hotel chain. A perfect, natural founder narrative.

Follow the timeline: listen for past tense and past perfect ('we had tried everything before it worked') as he narrates the turnaround.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Present perfect and past simple - 6 Minute Grammar

A short, clear guide to the exact grammar of this lesson: when to use the present perfect (open period: for, since, this year) and when to switch to the simple past (finished time: in 2019, last March) -- plus the passive voice for results. The clearest way to lock in the tense-switch and fix the habit of dropping "be".

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre a sua carreira -- uma frase no present perfect e uma no simple past com uma data.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Contracts & Agreements -- Legal English

Series
Suits -- a deal negotiation scene

Pick any negotiation scene from Suits where the lawyers walk through a contract. Listen for the legal modals of this lesson: what each party shall do, what the agreement must include, and what either party may do. Notice how the terms are stated in the passive ("the deal must be signed today", "the clause shall be removed"). It is the exact language you practiced, in a fast, real exchange.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com shall, uma com must e uma com may.

Find on Netflix ↗

Podcast
Planet Money — Surprise, you just signed a contract! How hidden contracts took over the internet

Planet Money explains how clicking 'I agree' online binds you to real contracts, including a rider who signed away his right to sue Airbnb — clear English on who is obliged to do what.

Notice the modal verbs of obligation ('you must', 'you agree to', 'are required to') that define each party's duties.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Legal English Vocabulary VV 26 - Contract Law (Lesson 1)

A short, clear guide to the exact grammar of this lesson: how shall states an obligation, must states a requirement, may states a permission, and how contracts use the passive voice for every term. The clearest way to lock in the legal modals and fix the habit of dropping "be" in the passive.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre um contrato seu -- uma obrigação, um requisito e uma permissão, com cada termo na passiva.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Data-Driven -- Describing Trends & Numbers

Series
Billions -- an earnings or results scene

Pick any scene from Billions where the team reviews performance and numbers. Listen for the language of this lesson: how one figure is higher than another, which is the highest, and how results are reported in the passive ("the target was hit", "the numbers were beaten"). It is the exact data language you practiced, in a fast, high-stakes exchange.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com comparativo, uma com superlativo e uma na passiva.

Find on Netflix ↗

Podcast
The Indicator from Planet Money — Examining the revisions in the May and June jobs reports

The hosts walk through the revised May and June jobs numbers with a former BLS commissioner, comparing the figures and explaining what the monthly report really tells us.

Track the comparison language ('higher than', 'revised down', 'compared with') as they read each figure.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Powerful Business English: How to Describe Charts, Graphs and Visuals in Presentations

A short, clear guide to the exact grammar of this lesson: comparatives and superlatives for figures, degree modifiers to say how big a change is (significantly, slightly, marginally), and the passive voice for reporting numbers. The clearest way to lock in the language of charts and fix the habit of dropping "be" in the passive.

Dica: pause após cada exemplo e diga a mesma ideia sobre um gráfico seu -- um comparativo, um superlativo e um número na passiva.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...

Série
Succession (HBO) -- the boardroom decision scenes

Watch the family and the board weigh options under pressure. Listen for how executives recommend a path (we should..., we ought to...) and how they warn about consequences (we had better..., or...).

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases -- uma com should ou ought to (conselho normal) e uma com had better (aviso com consequência).

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Make Better Decisions Under Pressure

A conversation on how leaders make hard calls: taking an expansive view of a situation, weighing pros and cons and trade-offs, and reaching a decision instead of falling back on habit. Rich input for the language of choices and reasoning.

Listen for how speakers compare options ('on one hand… on the other hand').

Open episode ↗

YouTube
English Grammar: Giving Advice – SHOULD, OUGHT TO, HAD BETTER

Short lessons that show the difference between normal advice (should / ought to) and strong advice with a consequence (had better), plus the base-verb rule and the negatives.

Dica: pegue uma decisão real do seu trabalho e grave uma recomendação em voz alta: uma frase com should ou ought to, e uma com had better + or...

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Houston, We Have Culture -- US vs Brazil

Série
Ted Lasso (Apple TV+) -- US vs UK culture clashes

Watch an American adapt to a very different work culture. Listen for how characters notice differences in directness, formality, and rapport, and how a warm mindset wins people over.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases que comparam culturas -- uma com whereas ou while, e uma com unlike ou different from.

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Cross-Culture Work in a Global Economy

INSEAD's Erin Meyer, author of The Culture Map, maps how communicating, scheduling (punctuality), leading (hierarchy), and disagreeing (directness) differ across countries, and how to build rapport with international partners.

Note the eight culture 'scales' she names; pick two that differ most for Brazil.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
The Museum of Fine Arts, Houston - Tour (4K)

Explore the Houston Museum District before your trip. Note how curators describe contemporary and abstract works -- perfect small talk to build rapport with US partners who share your taste in art.

Dica: depois de assistir, grave uma frase comparando o gosto americano e o brasileiro por arte, usando similar to e different from.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — The Big Review -- Block 2 Integration

Série
Industry (HBO) -- deals, terms, and negotiation

Watch how analysts and executives report results, state contract terms, and weigh trade-offs under pressure. A high-stakes showcase of every Block 2 structure in real business English.

Dica: assista a uma cena de negociação e anote uma frase de cada tipo -- present perfect, modal de obrigação, trend language, conselho e comparação.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Negotiate Better, Start with Yourself

William Ury, co-author of 'Getting to Yes' and originator of BATNA, shares methods for negotiating better outcomes: knowing your options and track record, weighing trade-offs, and giving a clear recommendation. Rich review of present perfect, modals, and advising language.

Listen for modal advice verbs (should, would, could) as he coaches negotiators.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED -- "The Art of Negotiation" (William Ury)

A clear talk on preparing your position, weighing options and trade-offs, and reaching agreement across cultures. Perfect to close Block 2 and prepare you to lead the meeting in Block 3.

Dica: depois de assistir, escreva cinco frases de auto-avaliação do Bloco 2, uma para cada estrutura, e leia em voz alta.

Watch on TED ↗

The Dream
Packing Words
The Code
Getting There
Practice
Your Turn
Wrap-up
Chapter 1 — The Dream
Maria Claudia at the Global Boardroom
B1+ — 60 minutes
Warm-up
Let us start with you.
"In one word: what excites you about running a holding company?"
"Tell me about a typical meeting with Houston."
Warm-up
Your English story
"What is the hardest part about speaking English in meetings?"
"When was the last time you wanted to say something in English but could not find the right word?"
Artifact
Executive Business Card
Curimbaba Holdings
Maria Claudia Curimbaba
Managing Director — Financial & Sales Operations
Sao Paulo, Brazil
3 Subsidiaries
Houston & Pittsburgh
Revenue +12% Q3
Let us build your executive vocabulary.
8 words that define your professional world
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Your Executive Vocabulary (1/2)
to manage from a high position
Oversee
to manage and supervise from a high position
"I oversee the financial operations."
a parent company
Holding (Company)
a parent company that owns other companies
"Our holding owns three subsidiaries."
owned by a parent company
Subsidiary
a company owned or controlled by a parent company
"The Houston subsidiary reports to us."
same role, different company
Counterpart
a person in the same position at another company
"My counterpart in Pittsburgh handles manufacturing."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Your Executive Vocabulary (2/2)
has an interest in the business
Stakeholder
a person with an interest in the business
"All stakeholders must approve the budget."
two companies sharing ownership
Joint Venture
a business arrangement between two companies sharing ownership
"We are planning a joint venture with a Texas company."
buying goods for a business
Procurement
the process of buying goods or services for a business
"The procurement process takes three months."
total income a company generates
Revenue
the total income a company generates
"Revenue increased by twelve percent."
0 / 4 words revealed
Pronunciation Drill
Stress Patterns
overSEE
/ˌoʊvərˈsiː/
HOLding
/ˈhoʊldɪŋ/
subSIdi-a-ry
/səbˈsɪdiˌeri/
COUNter-part
/ˈkaʊntərˌpɑːrt/
STAKEhol-der
/ˈsteɪkˌhoʊldər/
JOINT ven-ture
/dʒɔɪnt ˈventʃər/
proCUREment
/proʊˈkjʊrmənt/
REve-nue
/ˈrevəˌnjuː/
Key Expressions
Three expressions you need
"I am responsible for..."
Used to describe your duties
"I deal with..."
Used to describe what you handle regularly
"I am based in..."
Used to tell people where you work
Expression Drill
Listen and repeat
"I am responsible for the financial operations of the holding."
"I deal with procurement and vendor negotiations."
"I am based in the Sao Paulo headquarters."
Application
Make it yours
"I oversee _______________."
"I am responsible for _______________."
"I deal with _______________."
"I am based in _______________."
"I am currently working on _______________."
Now let us look at the grammar behind your words.
Present Simple vs Present Continuous
Chapter 3 — The Code
Observe the pattern
"I oversee the financial operations." (always)
"She deals with partners every month." (regularly)
"I am working on a joint venture." (now)
"We are planning an expansion." (current project)
Grammar Discovery
"What do the purple words have in common?"
TenseFormUse
Present SimpleI oversee / She dealsHabits, routines, facts
Present ContinuousI am working / We are planningCurrent, temporary actions
Signal words: Simple = always, usually, every day / Continuous = currently, right now, at the moment
Common Mistake
Spot the difference
"The meetings are doing by the internet."
"The meetings are held online."
Grammar Practice
Fill in the correct form
"She ___oversees three departments every day."
"We ___are planning a new joint venture right now."
"He ___deals with the Houston office every Friday."
"I ___am currently working on a procurement contract."
"The stakeholders ___expect quarterly reports."
Time to put it all in context.
Your first meeting in English
Chapter 4 — Getting There
Your first meeting in English
Artifact
Email from your counterpart
From: david.chen@pittsburgh-ops.com
To: mc.curimbaba@curimbaba-holdings.com.br
Subject: Looking forward to meeting you

Dear Maria Claudia,

I am David Chen, Operations Director at our Pittsburgh facility. I understand you oversee the financial and sales operations for the holding. I would love to learn more about your subsidiaries and discuss the upcoming joint venture.

Could we schedule a brief introduction at the conference? I am particularly interested in your procurement process and Q3 revenue results.

Best regards,
David Chen

Dialogue
Maria Claudia meets David Chen
DC
"Hi! I am David Chen from our Pittsburgh office. Are you the new managing director?"
MC
"Nice to meet you, David. I am Maria Claudia. I oversee the financial and sales operations of the holding here in Sao Paulo."
DC
"Great to meet you! What exactly do you handle on a daily basis?"
MC
"I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries. I also deal with procurement contracts and stakeholder relations."
DC
"That sounds like a lot! Do you work with the Houston team as well?"
MC
"Yes, I deal with my counterpart in Houston regularly. We are currently working on a joint venture together."
DC
"Impressive! How is the revenue looking this year?"
MC
"Our revenue increased by twelve percent last quarter. We are on track to meet our annual targets."
Dialogue Comprehension
Did you understand David?
1. Where is David Chen from?
David is from the Pittsburgh office.
2. What does David want to discuss?
He wants to discuss the joint venture and the procurement process.
3. What did David ask about the company performance?
He asked about how the revenue is looking this year.
Listening 1
Executive Introduction
Listen to an executive introducing herself at a conference.
0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the speaker oversee?
The financial and sales operations of a family holding company.
2. How many subsidiaries does she manage?
Three subsidiaries in different sectors.
3. What is she currently working on?
Expanding the company's joint ventures.
Listening 2
Meeting Opening
Listen to an executive opening a quarterly review meeting.
0:000:00
Speed
1. What are the three agenda items?
Q3 revenue numbers, joint venture proposal, and procurement timeline.
2. Who should have received the report?
All stakeholders.
More practice
From listening to speaking
Chapter 5 — Practice
From guided to free
Show what you have learned
Oral Drill
Respond to each situation
1. Introduce your role at the holding company.
"I oversee the financial and sales operations of our family holding company."
2. Describe what you are responsible for.
"I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries in different sectors."
3. Explain who you work with internationally.
"I deal with my counterparts in Houston and Pittsburgh on a regular basis."
4. Tell about the company revenue.
"Our revenue increased by twelve percent last quarter."
5. Describe your current project.
"I am currently working on a joint venture with a Texas-based partner."
6. Explain where you are based.
"I am based in Sao Paulo, and our headquarters oversees operations across Brazil and the US."
Quick Fire
Think fast
0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A VP at a global summit asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am Maria Claudia Curimbaba. I oversee the financial and sales operations of a family holding company based in Sao Paulo."
Spot the Error
Find the mistake
"She collaborate with many departments."
"She collaborates with many departments." (3rd person -s)
"I am currently work on a new project."
"I am currently working on a new project." (continuous = am + -ing)
"He oversee the sales department."
"He oversees the sales department." (3rd person -s)
"The meetings are doing by the internet."
"The meetings are held online." (passive: are held, not are doing)
"I am responsible of the budget."
"I am responsible for the budget." (responsible FOR, never OF)
0 / 5 errors found
Sentence Building
Unscramble the sentences
operations / I / the / financial / oversee
"I oversee the financial operations."
responsible / three / am / I / for / subsidiaries / managing
"I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries."
currently / on / venture / joint / working / a / am / I
"I am currently working on a joint venture."
with / deal / in / counterparts / I / Houston / my
"I deal with my counterparts in Houston."
increased / revenue / by / our / percent / twelve
"Our revenue increased by twelve percent."
Free Practice
No script, no safety net
"You are at a dinner with international investors. Stand up and introduce yourself and your company in 60 seconds."
"An investor asks: How is the business going? Respond with real numbers."
Your boardroom, your words
From guided to free production
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
The boardroom is yours
Role-Play: Guided
Houston office visit
Guided Role-Play
You arrive at the Houston office for the first time. The receptionist takes you to the conference room where your counterpart is waiting. Introduce yourself using all the keywords below.
Maria Claudia oversee holding three subsidiaries deal with procurement joint venture revenue +12%
Role-Play: Semi-Free
New partner meeting
Semi-Free Role-Play
You meet a potential new partner at a trade show. They know nothing about your company. Tell them what you do and why they should work with you.
Sao Paulo stakeholders twelve percent
Role-Play: Free
Board meeting opening
Free Role-Play
You are opening the quarterly board meeting. All stakeholders are present, including your counterparts from Houston and Pittsburgh. Open the meeting, set the agenda, and introduce the key topics. No script.
Error Correction
Let us fine-tune
Use this space to address errors you noted during the role-plays. Write them on a shared screen or whiteboard.
Teacher Notes Area
Write errors and corrections here during the lesson.
Reflection
Record your thoughts
"What did I learn today? What was easy? What do I need to practice more?"
Chapter 7 — Wrap-up
Survival Card
1
Let me introduce myself. I am Maria Claudia from Sao Paulo.
2
I oversee the financial and sales operations of our holding.
3
I am responsible for managing three subsidiaries.
4
I deal with international partners in Houston and Pittsburgh.
5
I am currently working on expanding our joint ventures.
What I Learned
Check your progress
I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know the 8 key vocabulary words for my business.
I can use Present Simple for habits and Present Continuous for current projects.
I can describe my role using "I am responsible for" and "I deal with."
I can discuss revenue and business operations with a counterpart.
I can open a meeting and set an agenda in English.
I understand the difference between "responsible for" and "responsible of."
I can pronounce all 8 vocabulary words correctly.
Day 1 — Complete
Homework: Record a 2-minute introduction of yourself and your company.

The Holding Structure — Describing Your Business Empire
